overview.body:
Your road trip game works great — but every time the program ends, all that progress disappears. Maria drove from San Antonio all the way to Houston, racked up 200 miles, survived two hazards, and then the program quit. Gone. Starting over from scratch is not a game. It's a punishment.

This week you fix that. You'll use Python's file I/O tools to write your game state to a text file and read it back when the player returns. By the end of this lab, a player can save mid-trip, quit the program, come back tomorrow, and pick up exactly where they left off.

mentorQuote.quote: Saves are what turn a session into a campaign. Without persistence, you don't have a game — you have a demo that forgets you exist. This week we make it remember.

objectives: Write game state to a text file using open() and write()
Convert numbers to strings with str() before writing
Read game state back from a file using readline() and rstrip()
Convert strings back to integers with int() after reading
Handle a missing save file gracefully using try-except FileNotFoundError
Add a main menu that lets players start fresh or load a saved game
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 6: Programs 6-1 and 6-4 (writing to files), Programs 6-3 and 6-5 (reading with readline and rstrip), Programs 6-6 and 6-7 (writing and reading numbers), Program 6-28 (try-except FileNotFoundError).
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 6
programRequirements: SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt' constant defined near the top of the file
save_game() function writes all 6 state variables to savegame.txt, one per line
load_game() function reads all 6 variables back and returns a tuple, or returns None if the file does not exist
Main menu at startup offers: 1. New Game, 2. Load Saved Game, 3. Quit
Game loop includes a Save and Quit option that calls save_game() before exiting

namedConstants.name: SAVE_FILE
namedConstants.description: String constant — filename for the save file, always 'savegame.txt'

overview.saveYourCode: Keep this file — Module 3.2 builds on it by adding lists and expanding the save format to include visited cities and hazards.

rows.task: Add SAVE_FILE constant and write save_game() function
Write load_game() with try-except and tuple return
Update program startup with New Game / Load / Quit menu
Add Save and Quit option inside the game loop
Test the full save-quit-reload cycle, then submit

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you are adding this week. Everything else carries over from your Lab 2-3 code.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANT ----
SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt'                              [NEW]
... (all distance and hazard constants from 2.3)

# ---- SAVE / LOAD FUNCTIONS ----
save_game(player_name, current_city, total_miles,
          total_turns, hazards_faced, hazards_escaped)  [NEW]
  └── writes 6 lines to savegame.txt

load_game()                                             [NEW]
  └── reads savegame.txt → returns tuple (or None)

# ---- HELPER FUNCTIONS (same as 2.3) ----
get_random_hazard()
display_destinations(current_city)

STARTUP:                                               [NEW]
│
│── Display main menu
│   ├── 1. New Game  → get player name, set starting city
│   ├── 2. Load Game → call load_game(), restore all vars
│   └── 3. Quit      → exit program
│
GAME LOOP: (same structure as 2.3)
│
│── Display status
│── Get destination choice
│── TRAVEL LOOP
│   └── Roll D20, move miles, check hazards
│── [NEW] Show Save and Quit option              [NEW]
│   └── if chosen: call save_game(), exit loop
│── Check win condition
│
FINAL TRIP REPORT (same as 2.3)

checkpoints.title: Add the SAVE_FILE Constant and save_game() Function

content.body: You are adding the ability to write your game state to a text file. The save_game() function takes the six variables that describe where the player is and what has happened, and writes them to savegame.txt — one value per line.

items: Start by copying your lab-2-3.py file to a new file named lab-3-1.py. All your work this week goes in lab-3-1.py.
Near the top of your file, after your imports and before the distance constants, add the new constant: SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt'
Define a new function save_game() that accepts six parameters: player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns, hazards_faced, and hazards_escaped.
Inside save_game(), open the save file for writing: outfile = open(SAVE_FILE, 'w')
Write each variable to the file as its own line. For string variables, write the value plus '\n'. For integer variables, wrap in str() first: outfile.write(str(total_miles) + '\n')
Write all six variables in this exact order: player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns, hazards_faced, hazards_escaped.
Close the file with outfile.close() and print 'Game saved!' to confirm.

references.description: Shows the basic open-write-close pattern for creating a text file.
Demonstrates writing user-provided strings to a file with '\n' appended.
Shows why numbers must be wrapped in str() before calling write().

content.title: write() only accepts strings
content.body: If you pass an integer directly to write(), Python raises a TypeError. You must convert every number before writing it: outfile.write(str(total_miles) + '\n')

items: SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt' constant defined near the top of your file
save_game() function defined with six parameters and a complete docstring
All six variables written to the file in order, with integers converted via str()
File closed after writing, and a confirmation message printed

content.body: You are writing the reverse of save_game(). The load_game() function opens savegame.txt, reads the six values back in the same order they were written, and returns them as a tuple. If the file does not exist, it returns None instead of crashing.

items: Define load_game() with no parameters.
Wrap the entire function body in a try-except FileNotFoundError block. This handles the case where no save file exists yet.
In the try block, open the file for reading: infile = open(SAVE_FILE, 'r')
Read each line in the same order it was written. For string variables, use readline().rstrip('\n'). For integer variables, wrap the readline in int(): total_miles = int(infile.readline())
After reading all six values, close the file with infile.close().
Return all six values as a tuple: return (player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns, hazards_faced, hazards_escaped)
In the except FileNotFoundError block, simply return None.

references.description: Shows how readline() reads one line at a time, including the '\n' character.
Demonstrates using rstrip('\n') to clean up lines read from a file.
Shows wrapping readline() in int() to convert file data back to integers.
Demonstrates try-except FileNotFoundError to handle a missing file gracefully.

content.quote: Order is everything in file I/O. Whatever order you write the lines — that is the exact order you have to read them back. Write name first, read name first. Mix up the order and your city becomes a number and your miles become a name. Test it.

items: load_game() function defined with a complete docstring
Entire function body wrapped in try-except FileNotFoundError
All six values read back in the same order they were written
Integer values converted with int() after reading
Returns a tuple of six values on success, or None if the file does not exist

content.body: Right now your program jumps straight into the game. This checkpoint adds a startup menu before the game loop so the player can choose to start a new game, load a saved game, or quit.

items: Before your game variable initialization, add the main menu display. Print the title, a separator, and three numbered options: 1. New Game, 2. Load Saved Game, 3. Quit.
Use your existing function_library.get_valid_int() to get the menu choice and validate it in the range 1–3.
If the player chooses 3, print a goodbye message and exit without starting the game. Use a return statement if your code is inside a function, or quit() at the top level.
If the player chooses 2, call load_game() and store the result. If the result is None, print 'No saved game found. Starting new game.' and fall through to the new game setup.
If load returned data (not None), unpack the tuple into your six game variables: player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns, hazards_faced, hazards_escaped = saved_data
Print a welcome-back message that shows the player's name and current city, then skip the new-game setup and go directly into the game loop.
If the player chooses 1, run the existing new-game setup: get their name, set current_city = 'San Antonio', initialize all stats to 0.

content.output:
============================================================
    DEEP IN THE HEART: A LONE STAR JOURNEY
    An All My Eggses Live in Texas Production
============================================================

MAIN MENU
--------------------
1. New Game
2. Load Saved Game
3. Quit

Choice (1-3):

content.title: Checking for None
content.body: The result of load_game() is either a tuple or None. Test it with if saved_data is None: — not if saved_data == None:. Using is is the correct Python style for None checks.

items: Main menu prints before any game variables are initialized
All three menu choices (New Game, Load, Quit) are handled
A successful load unpacks the tuple and restores all six variables
A failed load (None) falls through to new-game setup with a message
New-game path still works exactly as before

checkpoints.title: Add the Save and Quit Option

content.body: The player can now load a saved game — but they can't save one yet. This checkpoint adds a Save and Quit option inside the game loop. After each turn, the player can choose to save their progress and exit cleanly.

items: Inside your game loop, after the travel and hazard resolution for a turn, add a prompt asking whether the player wants to keep playing or save and quit. Use your existing function_library.get_yes_no() function.
If the player chooses to quit, call save_game() with all six current game variables.
After saving, print a confirmation message and break out of the game loop.
If the player chooses to keep playing, the loop continues as before.
Test the full cycle: start a new game, play one turn, save and quit, run the program again, choose Load Saved Game, and verify your stats are restored correctly.
Run the program. Choose New Game. Play one turn and travel to a destination.
When prompted to continue, choose to Save and Quit. Verify you see the save confirmation.
Open savegame.txt in a text editor. Confirm it has exactly 6 lines with your game data in the correct order.
Run the program again. Choose Load Saved Game. Verify your player name, city, and stats are restored exactly.
Play another turn, then let the game end normally without saving. Run it a third time and load — you should still be at the last saved state, not the post-game state.

content.title: What a good save file looks like
content.body: If you saved after traveling to Houston with 200 miles and 2 turns, your savegame.txt should look exactly like this:
items: Line 1: Maria
Line 2: Houston
Line 3: 200
Line 4: 2
Line 5: 1
Line 6: 0
Game loop includes a Save and Quit prompt after each turn
Choosing to quit calls save_game() with current values and breaks the loop
The full save-quit-reload cycle works correctly end-to-end
savegame.txt contains exactly 6 lines in the correct order

beforeYouSubmit: lab-3-1.py has a top-of-file comment block with your name, abc123, section number, and a description of the program
Both save_game() and load_game() have docstrings
SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt' constant is defined and used — no hardcoded filename strings inside the functions
load_game() uses try-except FileNotFoundError and returns None if no save file exists
Main menu at startup offers New Game, Load Saved Game, and Quit
Game loop includes a Save and Quit option that calls save_game() before exiting
All six variables are written and read in the same order
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 6 introduces open(), write(), readline(), rstrip(), str(), int(), and try-except FileNotFoundError. All techniques from Modules 1 and 2 remain available. Do NOT use the with statement (Chapter 6.3 — introduced in the library in Module 3.2), readlines(), list operations, or any Chapter 7+ features.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–6 (all techniques from Chapters 2–5 plus Chapter 6 file I/O and exceptions as introduced this week)
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: with statement, readlines(), list operations, sort(), append(), dictionaries, classes

warnings: Do not hardcode the filename 'savegame.txt' inside save_game() or load_game(). Use the SAVE_FILE constant — CodeGrade may rename the file to test flexibility.
Read back every value in the exact same order it was written. If save_game() writes player_name first, load_game() must read player_name first.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep lab-3-1.py — Module 3.2 builds directly on this file by adding list tracking for visited cities and hazards, and expanding the save format.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Programs 6-1 and 6-4 for the write pattern, and Programs 6-3 and 6-5 for the readline pattern
Open savegame.txt in Notepad after saving — if the file looks right but load fails, check your data types (integers must be wrapped in int())
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which tests pass or fail so you know where to focus
Come to Zoom or office hours — file I/O bugs are fast to spot on a screen share
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 6 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every file I/O program from the textbook
